# MAIN ARTICLE # Contents lists available at ScienceDirect # Sensors and Actuators: B. Chemical journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/snb # Rapid colorimetric detection of H2O2in living cells and its upstream series of molecules based on oxidase-like activity of CoMnO3nanofibers Zoujun Peng a,b , Yucheng Xiong c , Ziwen Liao a,d , Min Zeng b , Jiali Zhong a , Xiaomin Tang e,* , Ping Qiu a,f,** - a Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang330031, China - b Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang330031, China - c School of Mathematics and Computer Sciences, Nanchang University, Nanchang330031, China - d College of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University, Nanchang330031, China - e The Fourth Affiliated Hospital, Nanchang University, Nanchang330003, Jiangxi, China - f Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang330031, China # A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: CoMnO3nanofibers Oxidase-like activity Colorimetric detection Smartphone Cellular H2O2Uric acid Glucose # 1. Introduction Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), a reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in the redox control of biological activity, is created extracellularly in response to both internal and external stressors [1 -3]. The intracellular concentration of H2O2remains at a low steady-state level (roughly1 -100nM) in normally respiring eukaryotic cells, and is under strict control [3 -5]. Therefore, the changes in H2O2levels result in the breakdown of cellular redox equilibrium, which causes intracellular oxidative stress and damage to tissues and organ systems [5 -8]. Large quantities of H2O2in living cells have reportedly been linked to various illnesses, including diabetes [7], heart diseases [9], rheumatoid arthritis [10], and neurological conditions such as Parkinson ' s disease [11] and * Corresponding author. ** Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang330031, China. E-mail addresses: txm605@163.com (X. Tang), pingqiu@ncu.edu.cn (P. Qiu). # A B S T R A C T Most studies on nanozymes in recent years have focused on enhancing their catalytic activity and synthesizing directly, although this task is still complicated. Bimetallic oxides are particularly desirable due to their mixed valence and structural properties. In this study, bimetallic oxide nanofibers (CoMnO3NFs) were prepared using a quick and effective hydrothermal synthesis technique, and exhibited outstanding oxidase-like activity. The catalytic activity of CoMnO3NFs displayed an inhibitory response on exposure to H2O2, which was applied in detecting H2O2and its upstream molecules (glucose and uric acid). Based on this, a straightforward, affordable, and accurate colorimetric approach was developed by combining with3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine. Employing the oxidase activity rather than the traditional peroxidase activity to detect H2O2drastically reduces the detection time. This method was also successfully used for in situ quantitative monitoring of intracellular H2O2, as well as detecting uric acid and glucose levels in human serum. Moreover, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system built on self-developed software was developed, enabling the on-site detection of H2O2without laboratory instruments. This study established theoretical support for the valid design of bimetallic oxide nanozyme activity and might guide the development of detection systems for accurately analyzing biomarkers in foods and biochemical products. Alzheimer ' s disease [12]. It is important to measure the amounts of intracellular H2O2to fully understand the pathophysiology of these disorders. Many techniques are available for quantifying H2O2, including electrochemical analysis [13,14], colorimetric measurement [15,16], and fluorescence analysis [17,18]. The colorimetric assay has drawn the maximum attention because of its affordability, reaction speed, and real-time readings. Since the concept of nanoenzymes was originally proposed [19], the latest developments in nanoscience and nanotechnology have provided new alternatives for the design of nanomaterials with natural enzyme activities. Nanozymes have attracted the attention of scientists due to their significant advantages, including their high specificity, large surface area, ease of preparation, low cost, and easy storage [20,21]. Hence, Z. Peng et al. they have been thoroughly explored to develop new technologies covering clinical diagnosis, biosensing, drug delivery, small-molecule detection, and other fields [21 -23]. Among these, the synergistic effect between various metal valence states, which confers better oxidative activity and catalytic characteristics than monometallic oxides, has led to an increase in interest in polymetallic oxides [24]. Some excellent polymetallic oxides have recently been studied as nanoenzymes, including Co1.5Mn1.5O4with multifunctional mimetic enzymatic capabilities [25], CeVO4with cytochrome c oxidase properties [26], and FeWOX with strong peroxidase-like activity [27]. The key problem is their poor sensitivity and affinity for specific substrates compared with natural enzymes with extremely complicated protein structures. This has inspired numerous researchers to pursue the specific complex morphology of nanoenzymes. Despite significant advancements in the in-depth investigation of nanozyme catalytic sites, more research is needed to fully understand their potential for use in constructing sensing platforms. Due to being extremely unique, new nanoenzymes must be explored to use their potential fully. The majority of approaches for H2O2were established based on peroxidase-like nanozymes, but one major downside was that they were time-consuming. In this study, high-efficiency oxidase-like CoMnO3nanofibers (CoMnO3NFs) were synthesized (Fig.1a) and examined for their potential to function as a dependable colorimetric assay when combined with3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) for precisely monitoring the concentration of intracellular H2O2and detecting the content of H2O2-related molecules (uric acid and glucose) in serum. First, we demonstrated the successful hydrothermal development of CoMnO3NFs. The findings of the ensuing experiments showed that CoMnO3NFs, with remarkable stability and reusability, could catalyze the conversion of oxygen in solution into superoxide anion (O2 · ﹣ ), which oxidized colorless TMB to blue oxTMB ⋅ H2O2hindered the oxidase-like activity of CoMnO3NFs, which served as the basis for monitoring H2O2, uric acid, and glucose ⋅ H2O2is produced during the hydrolysis of these upstream molecules, which reduces the colorimetric response of the assay. The ability of CoMnO3NFs to detect intracellular H2O2and its upstream molecule levels in human serum was subsequently evaluated. Furthermore, a self-developed software program was designed and used to analyze the color characteristics (RGB values) and automatically calculate the H2O2concentration. This simplified colorimetric sensing device enabled the on-site H2O2measurement without the lab equipment, hence broadening the potential practical applications of nanosensors. # 2. Results and discussion # 2.1. Structural characterization of the CoMnO3NFs First, ultraviolet -visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectroscopy was used to identify CoMnO3NFs (Fig.S1). The distinctive peak was located at247and380nm, and the Tyndall effect was clearly visible in the inset. The fabrication of CoMnO3NFs was further examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Figs. 1b and S2, CoMnO3NFs preserved their nanofiber-like morphology and exhibited a1D morphological nanostructure with diameters ranging from20to40nm and lengths of a few micrometers. The fringes with the lattice spacing of0.271, 0.242, 0.222, and0.176nm were highlighted and assigned to the (121), ( GLYPH<0> 110), (120), and (220) planes of CoMnO3NFs, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 1c. The Co, Mn, O, and Cu elements in the synthesized CoMnO3NFs were visible in the product ' s EDS spectrum (Fig.1d). The source of the Cu element was generated from the carbon-coated copper grid. Fig. 1e -h shows the high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) image and the elemental mapping of the synthesized CoMnO3NFs, demonstrating a homogeneous distribution of Co, Mn, and O elements. The effective synthesis of CoMnO3NFs was further confirmed by Xray diffraction (XRD) (Fig.2a). The characteristic2 θ peaks from the XRD patterns at24.53 ◦ , 33.49 ◦ , 36.40 ◦ , 41.57 ◦ , 50.29 ◦ , 54.71 ◦ , and65.49 ◦ were assigned to (110), (121), ( GLYPH<0> 110), (120), (220), (231), and ( GLYPH<0> 211), respectively, which corresponded to CoMnO3 (JCPDF: 75 -2090). Furthermore, CoMnO3NFs had no visible impurity peaks, indicating that CoMnO3NFs with high purity were created. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate Fig. 1. (a) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of CoMnO3NFs. (b)TEM and (c) high-resolution TEM image of CoMnO3NFs. (d) EDS spectrum of CoMnO3NFs. HRTEM image of CoMnO3NFs (e) and the elemental mapping of CoMnO3NFs (f, g, h). Z. Peng et al. Fig. 2. (a) XRD spectrum of CoMnO3NFs. XPS characterization of CoMnO3NFs: (b) survey, (d) Mn2p, (e) Co2p, (f) O1s (c) FTIR spectra of CoMnO3NFs. the elemental composition of CoMnO3NFs. As shown in Figs. 2b and S3, it was found from the full spectrum of CoMnO3NFs that the binding energies corresponded to Co2p, Mn2p, Mn3s, Mn3p, and O1s, based on which it was calculated to the content of each element, suggesting all elements were present in CoMnO3NFs. Fig. 2e is the high-resolution Co2p XPS spectrum, in which the binding energies of780.4and795.3eV could be attributed to Co2p3/2and Co2p1/2of the Co3 + ion, while the binding energies of784.6and797.4eV refer to Co2p3/2and Co2p1/2of the Co2 + ion, respectively [25]. The binding energies of790.0and804.4eV corresponded to their satellites. The high-resolution Mn2p XPS spectrum (Fig.2d) could be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at642.0, 654.0, and643.0eV, which corresponded to Mn2 + , Mn3 + , and Mn4 + , respectively [28]. Additionally, the high-resolution O1s XPS spectra (Fig.2f) were deconvoluted into two peaks, with the peak at528.8eV being centered on Mn-O-Mn and the peak at531.5eV being attributed to surface-adsorbed water [29]. FTIR spectroscopy was employed to determine the identity of the surface functional groups of CoMnO3NFs. In the spectra (Fig.2c), the absorption bands at3420and2980cm GLYPH<0> 1were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O -H group and the stretching vibrations of C-H in -CH3, respectively [30]. Moreover, the absorbance band at1620cm GLYPH<0> 1corresponded to the C --C stretching vibrations, while the bands at1400, 1045, and880cm GLYPH<0> 1indicated C --O vibrations, C -O vibrations, and δ (CO3) in the CoMnO3NFs, respectively [24]. The characteristic vibrations of Co -O and Mn -O coordination bonds appeared at623and480cm GLYPH<0> 1 , respectively [13]. Accordingly, the characteristic vibration peaks of organic groups in CoMnO3NFs might be attributed to a small amount of acetate from the MnAc2precursor. # 2.2. Oxidase-like activity of CoMnO3NFs The exceptional oxidase-like properties of CoMnO3NFs were demonstrated by the fact that they could catalyze TMB effectively without the involvement of H2O2. According to a recent study [25], oxygen might have had some influence on the oxidase-like process, and the external environment of the reaction system (O2-, air-, and N2-saturated) might have affected the outcome. Comparisons were made between the outcomes of the catalytic reaction of CoMnO3NFs on TMB (from colorless to mazarine blue) under various external reaction conditions (Fig.3a). The reaction system with O2saturation produced the dark blue solution, which had the maximum UV absorbance at652nm. In contrast, the reaction system with N2saturation had the lowest absorption. The absorbance under air medium was between that under O2and N2media. After that, when the reaction system was saturated with air, various concentrations of CoMnO3NFs were added. Figs. 3b and S4showed a linear correlation between the concentration of CoMnO3NFs and the absorbance of oxTMB at652nm. The aforementioned findings demonstrated the effect of oxygen concentration in this process and the oxidase-like activity of CoMnO3NFs. In addition, the universality of oxidase activity of CoMnO3NFs for oxidizing colorimetric substrates was also investigated. The well-known chromogenic substrates OPD and ABTS were used for comparison with TMB in the same situation for experiments. Both visual inspection and spectral analysis showed that CoMnO3NFs effectively oxidized both OPD and ABTS like TMB (Fig.3c). It was crucial to understand that CoMnO3NFs catalyzed the decomposition of O2to produce specific types of ROS so as to demonstrate the reaction mechanism underlying the catalytic behavior. As scavengers of superoxide anion (O2 · ﹣ ), singlet oxygen ( 1O2), hydroxyl radicals ( · OH), and H2O2, the corresponding ascorbic acid (AA), tryptophan (Trp), isopropanol (IPA), and catalase (CAT) were added to the oxidase-like reaction system, respectively, and the change in the absorbance was measured at652nm. As shown in Figs. 3d and S5, the absorbance of the AA reaction system was much lower than that of the blank system. The outcomes showed that the main driving force behind the oxidase-like transformation of TMB into oxTMB was the generation of O2 · ﹣ during the catalytic process, which might originate from the dissolved oxygen bound to the oxygen vacancies in the CoMnO3. Wire- Z. Peng et al. Fig. 3. (a) UV -vis spectrum of TMB after being oxidized by CoMnO3NFs. (b) The absorbance values of TMB at652nm oxidized by different concentrations of CoMnO3NFs (the inset shows the visually recognizable color changes of the reaction system). (c) UV -vis spectrum of different systems. (d) Effects of different quenchers on the oxidation of TMB by CoMnO3NFs. (e) Mechanisms for the oxidase activity of CoMnO3NFs based on the TMB oxidation. shaped CoMnO3NFs could offer a large number of active sites for oxidase-like reactions that could not only fix the reactant on the surface of the nanofiber but also facilitate the transfer of electrons between TMB and oxTMB thanks to their large specific surface area and numerous nanolayered holes. Meanwhile, the redox couples provided by CoMnO3NFs, Co3 + /Co2 + , Mn3 + /Mn2 + , and Mn4 + /Mn3 + had an excellent synergistic effect that could significantly enhance the oxidase-like reaction. As a whole, the detailed oxidase-like catalysis mechanism of CoMnO3NFs is depicted in Fig.3e. According to Eqs. (1) -(3), O2was converted into O2 · ﹣ by accepting an electron from the lower valence of Co and Mn (Co2 + , Mn2 + , and Mn3 + ). On the contrary, the O2 · ﹣ oxidized the TMB molecules to generate oxTMB over the surface of CoMnO3NFs (Eq.(4)). As a result of the larger standard reduction potential of Co3 + /Co2 + ( E0 = 1.81V) compared with Mn3 + /Mn2 + ( E0 = 1.51V) and Mn4 + /Mn3 + ( E0 = 0.15V), Co3 + was then reduced to Co2 + by Mn2 + and Mn3 + (Eqs. (5) and (6)). Finally, Mn4 + and Mn3 + received one electron from the TMB and were reduced to Mn3 + and Mn2 + , respectively (Eqs. (7) and (8), respectively). Z. Peng et al. The batch stability and storage stability were examined to confirm the viability of CoMnO3NFs in real applications. After being kept for30days, the oxidase-like activity of CoMnO3NFs slightly decreased (Fig. S6), demonstrating that CoMnO3NFs could retain their original makeup and state even when kept for an extended period. The batch stability was assessed by measuring the absorbance at652nm in the CoMnO3NFs/ TMB system. The results (Fig.S7) revealed that all six batches of CoMnO3NFs possessed oxidase-like activity, demonstrating their high reproducibility. # 2.3. Kinetic analysis of CoMnO3NFs The concentration of TMB was closely related to the steady-state kinetic parameter during the CoMnO3NF oxidation-like reaction. The calculation of Michaelis -Menten parameters, which was essential for understanding the kinetics of the CoMnO3NF oxidase-like process, yielded the Lineweaver -Burk plots. where V denotes the initial velocity, [S] denotes the concentration of the TMB substrate, Vmax is the maximum reaction velocity, and Km denotes the Michaelis constant. The following Lineweaver -Burk diagram could provide information on the kinetic parameters Km and Vmax. By adjusting the concentration of TMB, the Michaelis -Menten kinetics and double reciprocal plot (Fig.S8) were used to analyze the catalytic activity of CoMnO3NFs. From the linear fitting in Fig. S9, Km and Vmax were calculated, as listed in Table1. Km has been shown to be a good predictor of an enzyme ' s substrate affinity, and a lower Km value implies a higher binding affinity for the substrate [31]. CoMnO3NFs displayed a higher affinity for TMB than for HRP, as evidenced by their apparent Km value being about one third that of HRP and their Vmax value being about seven times that of HRP [19]. Additionally, the Km value of CoMnO3NFs was relatively low, while its Vmax value was high compared with those of other monometallic oxides, indicating a higher catalytic affinity for TMB. The high affinity of CoMnO3NFs was a result of both the high number of active sites provided by the fiber-like structure and the synergistic interaction between Co and Mn. In conclusion, the aforementioned findings proved that CoMnO3NFs had superior oxidase-like catalytic capacity.

Table1The Km and Vmax values of the CoMnO3NFs and other oxidase-like catalysts for the substrate of TMB. # 2.4. Working principle of CoMnO3NFs for H2O2, UA, and Glu sensors After probing the oxidase-like catalytic activity, we continued to explore the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of CoMnO3NFs ⋅ H2O2is a crucial catalytic substrate for this activity. However, as illustrated in Fig. 4a, the absorbance at652nm in the system containing H2O2did not increase as predicted, but rather reduced sharply. Hence, we speculated that the oxidase-like catalytic activity of CoMnO3NFs might be disrupted by H2O2. The UV-Vis spectrum of CoMnO3NFs incubated with H2O2 (Fig.S10) revealed that a new absorption peak at205nm emerged in place of the characteristic absorption peaks at247and380nm. The FT-IR spectrum additionally demonstrated that the peaks in the fingerprint region also changed (Fig.4b). These findings suggested that the structural integrity of CoMnO3NFs may have been compromised by H2O2. Further TEM analysis of the morphology of CoMnO3NFs after H2O2incubation was performed (Fig.S11). It was discovered that their fiber-like structure changed to a seamless design and the nanopores between the single nanowires vanished, meaning they could not offer active sites for oxidase-like reactions. The alterations in morphology and structure resulted in less electron transfer between TMB and oxTMB, which decreased the absorbance peak. This motivated the development of a method to measure the concentration of H2O2using the oxidase-like catalytic activity of CoMnO3NFs (Fig.4c). H2O2, the main product of some oxidases [using the uricase and glucose oxidase (GOx) as enzyme-catalyzed reaction models], can act as a bridge to establish the quantitative analysis of the upstream series of molecules, which underlies a simple and sensitive colorimetric detection for uric acid and glucose (Fig.4c). The amount of H2O2produced was proportional to the concentrations of UA and Glu, and this relationship was reflected by the absorbance of oxTMB, which could determine the contents in experimental samples accurately and sensitively. The viability of this approach for the quantitative detection of UA and Glu was investigated. Different reagents were added to solutions of TMB individually, and the UV-Vis absorption response to these additions was recorded (Fig.4d). As anticipated, when UA and uricase were present together, the absorbance dropped due to the generation of H2O2, which etched CoMnO3NFs and reduced the active site to oxidize TMB. Similar results are shown in Fig. 4e, revealing that the absorbance only diminished when Glu and GOx were present together with CoMnO3NFs. Therefore, the oxidase-like activity of CoMnO3NFs could be used to quantitatively detect H2O2and its upstream molecules. # 2.5. Colorimetric detection of H2O2using the CoMnO3NFs-based assay Several experimental parameters were optimized to enhance the analytic performance for detecting H2O2based on CoMnO3NFs. The measure used to assess the ideal conditions was the change in absorbance ( Δ A ), which was computed using the equation Δ A = A0 -A . Generally speaking, the greater the change in absorbance, the more sensitive the detection. Herein, A0and A were the absorbance values of TMB oxidation by CoMnO3NFs in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively. Four parameters, including temperature, pH, time, and concentration of CoMnO3NFs, were optimized, since they all had a direct impact on the experimental outcomes when CoMnO3NFs and H2O2were incubated. As illustrated in Fig. S12a -d, the ideal incubation conditions included a temperature of40 ℃ , a pH of4.0, a duration of10min, and a concentration of CoMnO3NFs of50 μ g/mL. CoMnO3NFs will oxidize TMB by increasing its oxidation product oxTMB absorbance value after interacting with H2O2in order to react the H2O2content. Previous studies showed that this process occurred instantly. Therefore, for this phase, only an optimal concentration of TMB was needed. As shown in Fig. S12e, 1.25mM was selected as the optimum TMB concentration for H2O2sensing. The capacity to detect H2O2in a solution using the CoMnO3NFsbased approach was evaluated using the optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 5a, the absorbance of oxTMB steadily reduced as the Z. Peng et al. Fig. 4. (a) UV -vis spectra of TMB interacting with different substrates. (b) FTIR spectra of CoMnO3NFs in the absence and presence of H2O2. (c) The sensing mechanism of the CoMnO3NFs-based assay for H2O2, uric acid, and glucose. (d) and (e) The viability for the detection of UA and Glu. concentration of H2O2increased from0.4to40 μ M. The resulting linear regression equations were y = -0.00448x + 0.35405 ( R2 = 0.997) (Fig. 5b), from which the limit of detection (LOD) was derived to be0.24 μ M based on3 σ / s ( s was the slope of the curve, while σ was the standard deviation). This value was significantly less than the maximum limit of H2O2in food samples as per the CFR [37] and the EC [38]. The findings of the comparison between CoMnO3NFs and other previously reported techniques are displayed in Table S1. The CoMnO3NFs-based method had a lower LOD than the majority of previously developed methods, indicating that it offered a potential alternative to other technologies for accurately detecting H2O2. The UV-Vis absorption intensity of the CoMnO3NFs-based approach was examined by adding various interfering substances in the detection system in the presence and absence of H2O2 (5 μ M) to gauge the selectivity toward H2O2. No interference from these compounds was seen when the test solution contained K + , Na + , Ca2 + , Mg2 + , NH4 + , SO4 2 -, HPO4 2 -, CO3 2 -, Cl -, glycine (Gly), and glucose (Glu) at100times (500 μ M) (Fig.5c and d). However, glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys) interfered with the assay system at this level. Furthermore, when their concentration was20 μ M (Figs.S13and S14), they had no impact on the system. Fortunately, typical levels of GSH and Cys were around3.4 μ M and9.7 μ M [39,40], respectively, which were substantially lower than the levels of H2O2in human body. Therefore, substances, except for H2O2, did not significantly interfere with the results of the real sample assay. In conclusion, the suggested approach had high selectivity to detect intracellular H2O2. Z. Peng et al. Fig. 5. (a), (e) and (g) Sensitivity of determination of H2O2, UA and Glu, respectively. (b), (f) and (h) Relationship between the concentration of H2O2, UA or Glu and the absorbance intensity at652nm of the assay (The insert: digital images of reaction systems with different amounts of H2O2, UA or Glu). (c) and (d) The selectivity of the detection system (The concentration of H2O2was5 μ M, glutathione and cysteine were20 μ M, and other interfering substances were500 μ M). # 2.6. Detection of UA and Glu using the CoMnO3NFs-based assay system According to a previous study [41], the uricase-catalyzed oxidation step performed best after15min of incubation at pH8.5and37 ℃ . Under these conditions, subsequent experiments were then carried out using the procedures for detecting H2O2. The maximum absorbance of the oxTMB steadily reduced as the concentration of UA increased from0.6to200 μ M, as shown in Fig. 5e. The LOD of0.38 μ M was derived from the resulting linear regression equations as y = -0.00078x + 0.28952 ( R2 = 0.991) (Fig.5f). Table S2displays the results of a comparison between the sensing performance of CoMnO3NFs toward UA and that of other published techniques, indicating its superior performance. A mixture solution was incubated with a variety of possible interferents, including some inorganic ions, amino acids, and biological molecules, to further verify the specificity for UA. Fig. S15shows that these substances did not result in observable differences, except for vitamin C (Vc), dopamine (DA), GSH, and Cys. Subsequent tests on Vc, DA, GSH, and Cys (Fig.S16) showed that the monitoring system was unaffected by Vc and DA concentrations below15 μ M and GSH and Cys concentrations below20 μ M. In some related studies [42, 43], the concentrations of Vc and DA in serum were7.8 μ M and1 μ M, respectively. The concentrations of GSH and Cys in serum were also much lower than those of UA, indicating that none of the aforementioned substances would interfere with the actual sample assay and the devised assay had good selectivity. In addition, it was reported that the GOx-catalyzed oxidation was performed for30min of incubation at pH7.0and37 ℃ [44]. Therefore, H2O2was generated by incubating Glu under the aforementioned conditions; subsequently, experiments were carried out using the procedures for H2O2detection. Fig. 5g and h showed that the absorbance at652nm for oxTMB continuously decreased as the concentrations of Glu increased from0.5to60 μ M, with linear regression equation y = -0.00284x + 0.26905 ( R2 = 0.993) and the LOD was0.19 μ M. Galactose (Gal), sucrose (SUC), and xylose (Xyl), among other potential interferents as earlier, did not interfere with the detection system after following the same procedure (Figs.S17and S18), indicating the suggested assay had good interference resistance. The proposed glucose detection method was compared with previously reported ones in Table S3. # 2.7. In situ detection of H2O2released from living cells The spiked and recovery studies were performed to show the accuracy and precision of the procedure before in situ monitoring of cellular H2O2. Some western countries use H2O2as a food additive in milk [45]. However, too much H2O2residue may have harmful effects on the body. Therefore, accurately and quickly identifying H2O2in milk samples is crucial. Such commercial milk samples were diluted10times with Tris-HCl buffer (20mM, pH7.5) for studies after being centrifuged at10,000rpm to remove fat. As shown in Table S4, a good recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) were achieved. The aforementioned findings encouraged further intracellular research. CoMnO3NFs and TMB were assessed for their cytotoxicity using an MTT assay in two types of cancer cells (B16and HepG-2) and a kind of normal cells (HUVECs) as the model cell lines to confirm the sensor did not generate cellular toxicity. More than about80% of the cells survived after exposure to CoMnO3NFs for an extended period (even at a concentration of75 μ g/mL) (Fig.6a). Confocal laser scanning microscopy showed the cell morphology was fine, further demonstrating CoMnO3NFs did not cause aberrant cell alterations (Fig.S19). Fig. 6b showed the addition of TMB had little impact on cellular viability. Furthermore, CoMnO3NFs exhibited promising hemocompatibility (Fig. S20). These findings demonstrated the biocompatibility of CoMnO3NFs by showing no apparent cytotoxicity to the cells. Consequently, the monitoring of H2O2generated by cells was investigated. Fig. 6c depicted the recorded responses of the cells to the injection of PMA, which could lead to the generation of H2O2in the cells. CoMnO3NFs were employed to detect the produced H2O2released from B16cells, HepG-2cells, and HUVECs. The surplus CoMnO3NFs were then used to oxidize TMB, which resulted in a shift in absorbance at652nm. We employed96 × 10 4cells in total to measure the amount of H2O2generated by these cells, and the values were1.11, 1.26, and0.58 μ M, which were calculated based on the calibration curve. Additionally, the average number of H2O2molecules released by one cell ( N0) was estimated using Avogadro ' s constant (6.02 × 10 23mol GLYPH<0> 1 ). The results were6.96 × 10 11 , 7.90 × 10 11 , and3.64 × 10 11 , respectively, and these values agreed with previous findings [14,46,47]. Z. Peng et al. Fig. 6. MTT assay of the (a) CoMnO3NFs (1 -7are control, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150 μ g/mL of CoMnO3NFs, respectively) and (b) TMB (1 -6are control, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5mM of TMB, respectively). (c) Absorbance values for different kinds of cells treated with or without PMA. # 2.8. Analysis of UA and Glu in real samples Multiple human serum samples were used as the real samples for UA and Glu analyses in practical applications. The thiol-blocking reagent Nethylmaleimide (NEM) was introduced to remove the interference of sulfhydryl material in human serum [48]. Fig. S21a and c displays the colorful production images created using serum samples that were applied to the sensor. Their colors matched the amounts of UA and Glu shown in Fig. 5f and h. Additionally, the system ' s blue color faded when a specific amount of UA or Glu standard solution was added to the serum samples (Fig.S21b and d). As shown in Table2, the assay results for detecting UA and Glu in serum were comparable to those of biochemical analyzer. The fact that the lower RSD ( n = 3) showed the suggested method was successfully employed to determine the levels of UA and Glu in serum samples and that it might be useful in routine monitoring and clinical treatment. # 2.9. Visual detection of H2O2using the smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing system The digital image colorimetry combined with a smartphone was explored for the convenient and fast detection of H2O2to realize more portable sensing (Fig.7a). A self-developed software (H2O2recognizer) installed on a common smartphone was used as a signal reader and analyzer to quantify the image color signal, and the color parameters included red (R), green (G), and blue (B). The solution ' s image was taken and recognized, and an algorithm built into the software made a reasonable adjustment for ambient light to obtain the RGB value, which was then automatically calculated using the equation to determine the precise H2O2concentration. The G/B value linearly increased with the increase in the logarithm of H2O2concentration over the range of5 μ M to1M and the LOD was2.1 μ M (Fig.7b). We used the smartphoneassisted colorimetric sensing system to analyze H2O2in milk samples by the spike and recovery experiment to verify its reliability and practicability. As depicted in Fig. S22and Table S5, acceptable recoveries were observed through the sensing system. The use of smartphones for H2O2detection offered the benefits of low cost, portability, and ease of use, which opened the door for the creation of useful applications. # 3. Conclusions The novel CoMnO3nanofibers were prepared using a straightforward, efficient, and environmentally friendly hydrothermal approach. The as-prepared CoMnO3NFs exhibited excellent oxidase-mimicking activity and showed tremendous promise in fast tests. Additionally, we created a unique colorimetric sensor for the detection and monitoring of H2O2, uric acid, and glucose by integrating with TMB. The sensor was successfully used to quantify the levels of intracellular H2O2, uric acid, and glucose in human serum. Furthermore, we constructed a selfdeveloped software program that allowed us to capture images and measure the H2O2content in actual samples. This study was significant not only because it outlined the fabrication of a functional sensor for the quick and efficient detection of H2O2and its upstream molecules but also because it offered a novel approach for fusing the colorimetric method with artificial intelligence. Although the method is straightforward, its sensitivity should be increased and test strip -like sensor carriers should be developed. # CRediT authorship contribution statement Zoujun Peng: Investigation, Writing -original draft. Zoujun Peng is a4th year undergraduate student of Nanchang University, China, and will pursue directly PhD degree at University of Chinese Academy of Sciences. Now he has published a paper on Anal.Chim.Acta as the first author. His research focuses on nanobiomaterials, nano enzyme and chemical sensors. Yucheng Xiong received his bachelor ' s degree (2021) from Nanchang University, China and now is a2nd year M.D. student in the same university. His research focuses on virtual reality. Ziwen Liao is an undergraduate student of Nanchang University. His research focused on food analysis and synthesis of nano materials. Min Zeng is an undergraduate student of Nanchang University. Her research focused on cell biology. Jiali Zhong is an undergraduate student of Nanchang University. His research focused on serum samples treatment. Dr. Xiaomin Tang received his bachelor ' s degree (2002) from Jiangxi University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, China and now works in the fourth affiliated hospital. His research focused on clinic analysis. Prof. Ping Qiu received PhD degree of Food Science from Nanchang University, China in2006, and now working in the same university as a Group leader. Research areas include chemical sensors, nano enzyme, electroanalysis chemistry and chemometrics.
